coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based nanoclusters for sonodynamic therapy of bacterial infection enhanced by deep biofilm penetration and hypoxia alleviation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Tao
Xing, Haiyan
Xiong, Miao
Gu, Mengqin
Xu, Zhigang
Zhang, Lei
Kang, Yuejun
Xue, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Sonodynamic therapy (SDT) is emerging as a novel and effective treatment modality for antimicrobial applications. However, limited penetration of sonosensitizers and hypoxic infectious microenvironment have significantly restricted the therapeutic efficacy of drug-resistant bacteria. Herein, carbon dots (CDs)-based nanoclusters (MCHH) are facilely constructed by covalent assembly of Mn-doped CDs and simultaneous encapsulation of sonosensitizer hematoporphyrin monomethyl ether (HMME), followed by surface modification with hyaluronic acid (HA). MCHH not only prevents the agglomeration of hydrophobic HMME molecules, but also facilitates their deeper penetration into biofilm. Furthermore, MCHH can be degraded by the overexpressed hyaluronidase in Gram-positive biofilm, resulting in the sustained release of HMME for SDT. Meanwhile, MCHH with catalase-mimic activity contributes to hypoxia alleviation by catalyzing the decomposition of H2O2 into O2. Under ultrasound irradiation, intense SDT can be achieved by MCHH, which is enhanced by its high biofilm permeability and local oxygenation. The proposed strategy produces superb bactericidal effect and realizes admirable biofilm disintegration outcome in vitro. On this basis, MCHH administration on bacterial wound infection model expedites wound healing, benefiting from the synergistic action of biofilm eradication, inflammation suppression, increased collagen decomposition and angiogenesis induction. Taken together, this paradigm provides strategic opinions in augmenting SDT efficacy for anti-infective applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterizations. TEM images of (a) CDs and (b) Mn-CDs. (c) HRTEM image of Mn-CDs. (d) AFM image of Mn-CDs. (e) TEM image of MCHH. (f) SEM image of MCHH. (g) Hydrodynamic size distributions of Mn-CDs and MCHH measured by DLS. (h) Zeta potentials of CD, Mn-CDs, MCH and MCHH. (i) XRD patterns of Mn-CDs and MCHH. (j) Full-survey XPS spectrum of MCHH. Core-level XPS spectrum of (k) manganese, (l) carbon and (m) nitrogen in MCHH. (n) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of MCHH. (o) 3D fluorescence spectrum of MCHH. (p) UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra of Mn-CDs, HMME and MCHH (inset: photograph of sample-laden vials).

HAase-responsive HMME release and US-triggered ROS generation. (a) Optical absorption spectra of the releasing medium where a dialysis bag loading HMME or MCHH (1 mg mL−1) in the presence of HAase (200 μg mL−1) was submerged in for 48 h. (b) Optical absorption spectra of the MCHH dispersion (1 mg mL−1) in a dialysis bag in the presence of HAase (200 μg mL−1) after incubation for 48 h. Fluorescence spectra of the releasing medium where a dialysis bag loading (c) HMME (1 mg mL−1) or (d) MCHH (1 mg mL−1) in the presence of HAase was submerged in during the incubation for up to 24 h. (e) Zeta potentials of various sample combinations after incubation for 12 h. (f) Working mechanism of MCHH for the enhanced 1O2 generation during sonosensitization. (g) Dissolved oxygen level in the mediums containing a variety of agents in the incubation period of 525 s. (h) Dissolved oxygen level in the mediums containing CDs or Mn-CDs at a steady temperature of 25 or 37 ℃ for 210 s (inset: photographs of CDs and Mn-CDs dispersed in H2O2 at 37 ℃). (i) ESR spectra to confirm the presence of 1O2 subject to different treatments upon the addition of the spin trap of TEMP. (j) Detection principle of SOSG for probing 1O2 radicals. (k) Fluorescence spectra of SOSG after various regimens (λex = 488 nm). (l) Fluorescence intensity of SOSG containing different agents during US irradiation for up to 10 min. (m) Operation mechanism of DPBF for 1O2 detection. (n) Decay of DPBF absorbance at 417 nm after various administrations. (o) Optical absorption spectra of DPBF solution containing MCHH and H2O2 after US irradiation for diverse periods. MCHH and H2O2 concentrations were set to be 200 μg mL−1 and 1 mM, respectively, and US irradiation (1.0 MHz, 1.0 W cm−2, 50 % duty cycle) was carried out for 10 min for panel (e)-(o).

Antibacterial effect in vitro. (a) Schematic diagram of the breakdown of giant liposomes after interaction with MCHH under US irradiation. (b) Microscopic images of giant liposomes after various treatments in the presence of H2O2 (1 mM) (scale bar: 5 μm). Groups are assigned to be (1) US, (2) MCHH, (3) MCHH + US. (c) Operating mechanism of MCHH in causing the damage of lipid bilayer in giant liposomes. (d) Digitalized photographs of the agar plates inoculated with SA or MRSA that were pretreated by various agents. Cell viabilities of (e) SA and (f) MRSA by quantifying the colony formation units corresponding to panel (d). Groups are allocated to be (1) Blank, (2) H2O2, (3) Mn-CDs + H2O2, (4) HMME + H2O2, (5) MCHH + H2O2 for panel (d)-(f). Growth curves of (g) SA and (h) MRSA subject to the initial treatment of MCHH at different concentration in the presence H2O2 (1 mM), followed by US irradiation for 10 min based on turbidimetry. Survival rates of (i) SA and (j) MRSA at 9 h corresponding to turbidimetry analysis. US irradiation (1.0 MHz, 1.0 W cm−2, 50 % duty cycle) was carried out for 10 min in all panels where applicable. Data are shown as mean ± SD (n = 4). *** p < 0.001.

Antibacterial mechanisms by MCHH. (a) Fluorescence microscopic images of MRSA after various treatments, followed by staining with FDA and PI (scale bar: 50 μm). (b) DNA and (c) protein contents that were released into extracellular region after different administrations. (d) The released ATP from bacterial cells after diverse regimens. (e) Relative GSH level after incubation with MCHH at different concentrations for 6 h. (f) Fluorescence microscopic images of MRSA after various treatments, followed by labeling with DCFH-DA probe (scale bar: 20 μm). (g) Fluorescence microscopic images of SA and MRSA after manifold regimens, followed by staining with SOSG probe (scale bar: 20 μm). Groups are assigned to be (1) MCHH (-)/US (-)/H2O2 (-), (2) MCHH (-)/US (+)/H2O2 (-), (3) MCHH (-)/US (-)/H2O2 (+), (4) MCHH (-)/US (+)/H2O2 (+), (5) MCHH (+)/US (-)/H2O2 (-), (6) MCHH (+)/US (-)/H2O2 (+), (7) MCHH (+)/US (+)/H2O2 (-), (8) MCHH (+)/US (+)/H2O2 (+) for panel (a) and (f). Groups are allocated to be (1) Saline, (2) H2O2 + US, (3) Mn-CDs + H2O2 + US, (4) HMME + H2O2 + US, (5) MCHH + H2O2 + US for panel (d). Groups are divided into (1) Saline, (2) Mn-CDs + H2O2 + US, (3) HMME + H2O2 + US, (4) MCHH + H2O2 + US for panel (g). US irradiation (1.0 MHz, 1.0 W cm−2, 50 % duty cycle) was carried out for 10 min·H2O2 dosage was set to be 1 mM. Data are shown as mean ± SD (n = 4). *** p < 0.001.

Bacterial uptake and biofilm permeation of MCHH. (a) Schemic diagram to illustrate the deeper biofilm penetration by MCHH. (b) Quantitative fluorescence intensity in HMME- or MCHH-treated MRSA cells based on flow cytometry analysis. (c) Bright field and dark field microscopic images of MRSA cells after treatment with MCHH for different periods (scale bar: 20 μm). Confocal microscopic images of at different scanning depths after MRSA biofilm receiving the administrations of (d) HMME and (e) MCHH for 6 h (scale bar: 20 μm). Normalized intensities of (f) HMME and (g) MCHH corresponding to panel (d) and (e) in terms of different scanning depths. 2.5 D confocal images of MRSA biofilm after treatment with (h) HMME and (i) MCHH in terms of different Z-stack positions.

Biofilm eradication in vitro. (a) 3D confocal microscopic images of SA biofilm after various treatments, followed by staining with FDA and PI. (b) Confocal microscopic image of MRSA biofilm, followed by staining with FDA. Biofilm formation rates of (c) SA and (d) MRSA after treatment with MCHH at different concentrations in the presence of H2O2, without or with US irradiation. (e) SEM images of MRSA biofilm succumbing to various administrations. (f) Biomass, (g) thickness, (h) dry weight and (i) formation rate of MRSA biofilm in terms of different treatment groups. Groups are allocated to be (1) Blank, (2) H2O2 + US, (3) Mn-CDs + H2O2 + US, (4) HMME + H2O2 + US, (5) MCHH + H2O2 + US for panel (a). Groups are assigned to be (1) Blank, (2) US, (3) H2O2 + US, (4) Mn-CDs + H2O2 + US, (5) HMME + H2O2 + US, (6) MCHH + H2O2 + US for panel (b) and (e)-(i). US irradiation (1.0 MHz, 1.0 W cm−2, 50 % duty cycle) was carried out for 10 min·H2O2 dosage was set to be 1 mM. Data are shown as mean ± SD (n = 4). *** p < 0.001.

Antibacterial and would healing performance of MCHH in vivo. (a) Schematic diagram to illustrate the bacterial killing effect by virtue of the multi-catalytic activities of MCHH. (b) Timing chart to illustrate the treatment procedure of mouse models with the establishment of MRSA wound infection. (c) Digitalized photographs of wound area on day 0, 1, 4, 7 and 10 after receiving various administrations (scale bar: 0.5 cm). (d) Wound area changes within the treatment period of 10 days. (e) Numerical simulation to quantify the wound area at the time points corresponding to panel (c). (f) Digitalized photographs of bacterial colonies on agar plates which were inoculated with wound tissue homogenate dilutes on day 10 (scale bar: 1 cm). Histological analysis of the infected skin wound tissue harvested on day 10 through staining of (g) H&E, IL-6, TNF-α, and (h) Masson, CD31, VEGF (scale bar: 100 μm). Groups are allocated to be (1) Saline, (2) H2O2 + US, (3) Mn-CDs + H2O2 + US, (4) HMME + H2O2 + US, (5) MCHH + H2O2 + US. US irradiation (1.0 MHz, 1.0 W cm−2, 50 % duty cycle) was carried out for 10 min·H2O2 dosage was set to be 1 mM.
Illustration diagram to display the synthetic procedure of MCHH and its application for the sonodynamic therapy of bacterial infection enhanced by deep biofilm penetration and hypoxia alleviation.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based nanoclusters for sonodynamic therapy of bacterial infection enhanced by deep biofilm penetration and hypoxia alleviation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sonodynamic therapy (SDT) is emerging as a novel and effective treatment modality for antimicrobial applications. However, limited penetration of sonosensitizers and hypoxic infectious microenvironment have significantly restricted the therapeutic efficacy of drug-resistant bacteria. Herein, carbon dots (CDs)-based nanoclusters (MCHH) are facilely constructed by covalent assembly of Mn-doped CDs and simultaneous encapsulation of sonosensitizer hematoporphyrin monomethyl ether (HMME), followed by surface modification with hyaluronic acid (HA). MCHH not only prevents the agglomeration of hydrophobic HMME molecules, but also facilitates their deeper penetration into biofilm. Furthermore, MCHH can be degraded by the overexpressed hyaluronidase in Gram-positive biofilm, resulting in the sustained release of HMME for SDT. Meanwhile, MCHH with catalase-mimic activity contributes to hypoxia alleviation by catalyzing the decomposition of H2O2 into O2. Under ultrasound irradiation, intense SDT can be achieved by MCHH, which is enhanced by its high biofilm permeability and local oxygenation. The proposed strategy produces superb bactericidal effect and realizes admirable biofilm disintegration outcome in vitro. On this basis, MCHH administration on bacterial wound infection model expedites wound healing, benefiting from the synergistic action of biofilm eradication, inflammation suppression, increased collagen decomposition and angiogenesis induction. Taken together, this paradigm provides strategic opinions in augmenting SDT efficacy for anti-infective applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) poses an enormous global threat to public health and economy for centuries [1,2]. Despite the decent bactericidal outcome by administering antibiotics, therapeutic effectiveness is still restricted by the generation of antibiotics-resistant pathogens in clinics [3,4]. Fundamental mechanisms of AMR include enzymatic degradation of antibiotic molecules, alternation of the antimicrobial target proteins, change in membrane permeability, as well as the development of drug efflux pumps [5–7]. More importantly, more than 80 % of bacterial infections are associated with biofilms, which are microbial communities incorporating self-produced extracellular substances [8,9]. The dense and heterogeneous biofilm can protect the residing bacteria against antibiotics, drug permeation and host immune response [10,11]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop robust antibacterial agents and antibiofilm strategies without causing drug resistance. Among the antibiotics-free and non-invasive approaches, sonodynamic therapy (SDT) is becoming a burgeoning modality for drug-resistant biofilm eradication, thanks to its deep tissue penetration, reliable biosecurity and excellent spatiotemporal modulation [12–15]. Upon the low-intensity ultrasound irradiation, sonosensitizers can transfer energy to surrounding oxygen and produce reactive oxygen species (ROS), mainly in the form of 1O2 [16]. However, clinically-used organic sonosensitizers, such as hematoporphyrin monomethyl ether (HMME), always suffer from the drawbacks of complex synthesis, poor aqueous solubility and limited biofilm permeability, which significantly impairs their SDT performance in biofilm clearance [17,18]. Furthermore, the characteristic of hypoxia in most biofilms not only facilitates inducing antibiotic resistance but also decreases sonosensitization efficacy [19,20]. To this end, new-style nanocarriers are desperately in demand to enhance the permeability of organic sonosensitizers in biofilm and simultaneously alleviate local hypoxia to comprehensively promote SDT.

Carbon dots (CDs) possess the advantages of easy preparation, high aqueous dispersity, good chemical stability, decent biosecurity and low cost, which have attracted increasing attention as biosensing agents and/or drug carriers toward multifunctional theranostic systems [21–23]. Also, the synthesis of CDs from man-made or natural carbon sources can bring along CDs with many chemical groups on particle surface (e.g., NH2,COOH and OH) [24,25]. Simultaneously, as-customized functional groups possess the ability to chelate with metals and enable CDs as building blocks for constructing large-assembly [26,27]. For example, several essential metal elements in our body (Fe, Cu, Zn, Mg, Co, Ca, etc.) have been elected as doping agents to enhance the physiochemical properties of CDs [28,29]. Metal ion-doped CDs generally showed better catalytic performance as compared to the pristine metal or CDs. The introduction of metal ions in CDs can influence the spin density and charge distribution, which in turn increases the number of catalytically active sites for binding substrates. In another aspect, high biocompatibility of CDs-based theranostic platforms is also appealing to clinical translations, in terms of the predictable and definite pharmacokinetics, specific and selective biodistribution, evasion of immune responses and interaction with biomolecules in body [30,31]. Surface functionalization with ligands could further improve the biocompatibility of CDs to offer attractive potential for marketable applications [32]. Therefore, antimicrobial agent-loaded metal-doped CDs is conceived to be promising for the treatment of bacterial infections.

Multivalence manganese (Mn)dopantsin their nanoforms occupy peroxidase-mimic, catalase-mimic and glutathione (GSH)-depletion activities [33–35]. For example, MnO2-based nanocomplex can effectively mitigate hypoxia through decomposing H2O2 into O2, which significantly benefits O2-dependent SDT or phototherapy, radiotherapy and chemotherapy [36–38]. In another aspect, GSH peroxidase (GPx)-like activities of Mn3+/4+ doped nanocrystals enable GSH consumption to disturb redox homeostasis in cytosolic region [39,40]. Moreover, Mn is an essential trace element that is incorporated in enzyme synthesis and regulate the metabolism of biomacromolecules in human body. In addition, the definite clearance and excretion pathways of Mn element promise the biosafety of Mn dopants in practical applications. The extracellular polymeric substances in biofilm matrix create a unique microenvironment with the features of hypoxia, mild acidity (pH 4.5–6.5) over-produced H2O2 (50–100 μM) and high-level GSH (10 mM) [41–45]. To this end, Mn-dopants could probably respond to these physiochemical clues in biofilm and remodel the microenvironment to be more sensitive to the treatment of nanoantibiotics. On this basis, Mn-doped CDs as drug carriers would endow pristine CDs with multi-catalytic activities to trigger O2 production and GSH elimination in lesion microenvironment, aiming to amplify the biofilm eradication efficacy [46–48].

Taking account of the above circumstance, in this study, a new type of CDs-based nanoclusters (MCHH) is constructed by covalent assembly of Mn-doped CDs and simultaneous high-efficient encapsulation of sonosensitizer HMME, followed by surface modification with hyaluronic acid (HA) (Scheme 1 ). Mn-doped CDs not only prevent the agglomeration of hydrophobic HMME molecules, but also facilitate their deeper penetration into biofilm. After administration of MCHH on biofilm, the conjugated HA can be degraded by the overexpressed hyaluronidase (HAase) in Gram-positive bacteria, resulting in the sustained release of HMME. Meanwhile, MCHH with catalase-mimic activity catalyzes the decomposition of H2O2 to O2. Such local oxygenation accompanied with deep permeation of sonosensitizers significantly augments HMME-mediated SDT efficacy. On the other hand, GSH-depletion by MCHH further aggravates redox imbalance in bacterial cells, resulting in superb biofilm disintegration outcome. In this paradigm, morphological features, structural characteristics and catalytic performance of MCHH are systematically explored in multiple perspectives. Antimicrobial activities toward bacterial suspension, bacterial plate and biofilm are demonstrated under different activating conditions. Based on these findings in vitro, MCHH treatment on bacterial wound infection model is carried out to investigate the biofilm eradication efficacy, inflammation level, collagen decomposition status and angiogenesis rate. At last, underlying mechanisms of anti-infection effect by MCHH toward Gram-positive pathogenic bacteria is in-depth analyzed and discussed.

Mn-CDs were first synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method based on using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as the precursor and manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O) as the manganese source. Similar to pristine CDs, as-prepared Mn-CDs exhibited representative spherical structure with high monodispersity, and the average size was measured to be ∼ 7.6 nm from transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 1 a and b). High-resolution (HR) TEM further revealed the lattice fringe of Mn-CDs with a d-spacing of 0.26 nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite (Fig. 1c) [49]. Such tiny-sized spherical structure of Mn-CDs was further validated through atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Fig. 1d). Mn-CDs displayed excitation wavelength-independent fluorescence emission and the maximum was confirmed at the wavelength of 621 nm, manifesting its potential photoluminescent properties (Figure S1). To synthesize MCHH, HMME molecules were covalently conjugated with the synchronous assembly of Mn-CDs based on the amine coupling through 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), followed by the surface activation with HA. As-prepared MCHH exhibited solid spherical structure with relatively uniform size distribution from TEM (Fig. 1e). Such morphology of MCHH was also visualized from scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and AFM, and the average size was recorded to be ∼ 55.7 nm (Fig. 1f and S2).

Hydrodynamic sizes of Mn-CDs and MCHH were measured to be 4.85 nm (polydispersity index [PDI]: 0.121) and 68.1 nm (PDI: 0.151), respectively, from dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. 1g). Compared with the geometric diameter of dehydrated particles, the slightly larger hydrodynamic sizes can be arising from the Brownian motion near the hydration layer at the solid–liquid interface [50]. Long-term aqueous stability of MCHH under different physiological environments was explored by continuously measuring its hydrodynamic size. No conspicuous size fluctuation of MCHH was observed in the mediums of deionized (DI) water, phosphate buffer saine (PBS) and fetal bovine serum (FBS, 10 %) during the monitoring period of seven days, testifying to the splendid stability of MCHH for prolonged blood circulation (Figure S3). Zeta potentials of Mn-CDs@HMME (MCH) and MCHH were determined to be 1.44 and −4.02 eV, respectively (Fig. 1h). After introduction of MCHH on biofilm, HAase secreted from Gram-positive bacteria can degrade the conjugated HA on nanoparticles, leading to the production of positively-charged MCH. In general, extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) matrix of biofilms has an overall negative net charge [51]. Moreover, hydration channels rich in biofilms for delivering water and nutrients are also capable of transporting nanoparticles [52]. Thus, MCH is very likely to be adsorbed by EPS to realize a deep biofilm penetration.

Crystalline forms of Mn-CDs and MCHH were characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD). XRD patterns of these two products showed the diffraction peak at ∼ 26°, which is assigned to the low-density amorphous graphite carbon structure (Fig. 1i). There was no obvious difference in the XRD patterns between Mn-CDs and MCHH, indicating that HMME encapsulation and HA modification did not cause notable change in the molecular orientation features of Mn-CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was exploited to analyze the elemental composition and valence states in MCHH. The characteristic peaks of C 1 s (285.06 eV), N 1 s (399.29 eV), O 1 s (532.41 eV) and Mn 2p (641.97) in the full-survey XPS spectrum of MCHH indicated the existence of these four elements (Fig. 1j). Core-level XPS spectra of Mn reveals the valence states of Mn2+, Mn3+ and Mn4+, positioning at 641.0, 642.1, 643.6 eV and 653.0, 653.6, 654.6 eV for the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 spinorbit split peaks, respectively (Fig. 1k). The multivalence state of Mn dopants is beneficial to the catalytic H2O2 decomposition and GSH scavenging. The C 1 s spectrum can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.78, 286.18, 287.08, and 288.88 eV, which are ascribed to C-C, C-N/C-O, C = C, O-C = O bonds, respectively (Fig. 1l). The binding energy peaks of 399.08 and 400.38 eV in the N 1 s spectrum belong to graphite N and ph-NH2, respectively (Fig. 1m). Core-level XPS spectra of O 1 s displayed two characteristic peaks at 532.1 and 533.5 eV, which are attributed to C = O and C-OH bands (Figure S4). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was exploited to resolve the functional chemical group in MCHH. The characteristic bands at ∼ 1556 and ∼ 1611 cm−1 belong to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of amide and C = O in HA, substantiating the effective HA coating on MCHH (Figure S5).

Taking account of the fluorescence property of HMME, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of MCHH were recorded in its aqueous form (Fig. 1n). The maximum fluorescence emission was reflected by the shoulder peaks at 616 and 679 nm, which is in good accordance with that of free HMME (Fig. 1o). The relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) was not significantly fluctuated during excitation for 10 min, verifying a high photostability of MCHH for potential applications in fluorescence imaging (Figure S6). UV–vis absorption spectrum of MCHH exhibited two characteristic peaks at 392 and 440 nm, which are assigned to HMME and Mn-CDs, respectively (Fig. 1p). Based on the spectrophotometry, the loading content of HMME in MCHH was calculated to be as much as 18.25 %, according to the standard curve (Figure S7 and S8). Meanwhile, the doping content of Mn in MCHH was quantified to be 10.14 % from inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES).

Bacterial HAase, enzymes capable of degrade HA, are produced by a number of pathogenic Gram-positive bacteria. Thus, surface modification with HA in MCHH not only protects healthy tissue from adverse effects but also causing selective cytotoxic effects against pathogenic bacteria, relying the specific functional activity through HAase-catalyzed HA degradation. In this regard, HAase-triggered HMME release from MCHH was first evaluated by using a standard dialysis method (Fig. 2 a). Optical absorption spectra of the releasing medium where a dialysis bag loading HMME or MCHH was submerged in were measured after 48 h incubation. In the presence of HAase, only a small amount of HMME was release from the dialysis bag, as evidenced by the weak fluorescence intensity at 616 nm, which can be attributed to the bulk aggregate formation from hydrophobic drug molecules under aqueous condition. By contrast, much stronger fluorescence emission was observed in the group of MCHH, indicating an efficient HMME release from the dialysis bag. Notable, such fluorescence became significantly weakened upon the addition of HAase pre-denatured at 100 ℃, implying HAase-specific responsiveness in terms of the drug release. Two sequential events may get involved in HAase-triggered HMME release. First, surface HA of MCHH was degraded by HAase to expose the nanoclusters of MCH. Next, HAase can also act as a hydrolytic enzyme to cleave amino bonds and trigger the dissolution of HMME. Such HAase-responsive HMME release can avoid pre-matured drug leakage and circumvent non-specific tissue adsorption. Furthermore, HMME release rate from MCHH in the presence of HAase was strongly relevant to ambient temperature (Fig. 2b). The most efficient HMME release was achieved at the temperature of 37 ℃, resulting from highest enzymatic activity of HAase. Afterwards, drug release behavior was further investigated by varying the incubation time from 0 h to 24 h (Fig. 2c and d). Fluorescence intensity at 616 nm was progressively increased over time in the group of “MCHH + HAase”, indicating a representative time-dependent HMME release manner (Fig. 2d). The high sustainability of drug release is of high practical potential to obtain a long-term antibacterial effect. To validate the surface charge reversal of MCHH by HAase, zeta potentials of MCHH were measured before and after incubation with HAase (Fig. 2e). As expected, positively-charged surface of MCHH was attained after reaction with HAase, which is highly favorable for penetration into the biofilm containing negatively charged EPS matrix.

Excessive production of ROS can cause damage to lipids, proteins and DNA in cytosolic region, which leads to progressive oxidative damage and ultimately bacterial death [53,54]. Thus, the burst of oxidative stress by SDT is a promising strategy to rapidly kill pathogenic bacteria. In another aspect, higher metabolic levels in the wound infection region disrupt the normal H2O2 and redox balance, which is accompanied by bacterial anaerobic glycolysis and the promotion of acidic anaerobic microenvironment. And, HMME-mediated sonosensitization is ascribed to the conversion of ground state atomic oxygen into singlet state by equilibrated hot electrons. This is an oxygen consuming process, which can be severely deactivated by the hypoxia in infection sites (Fig. 2f). Therefore, it is requisite to reverse the hypoxic status to improve the SDT efficacy. Fortunately, the catalase-like activity of Mn (II/III) couples can contribute to the decomposition of H2O2 and cause local oxygenation. It the light of this, the catalytic activity of MCHH was evaluated by monitoring the dissolved level in H2O2 solution for 525 s. Compared with the other groups, Mn-CDs and MCHH led to an immediate increase of dissolved O2 level in the initial 200 s, indicating their capability in decomposing H2O2 for oxygen replenishment (Fig. 2g). In addition, the catalase-like activity of Mn-CDs was strongly dependent on the ambient temperature, and O2 generation at 37 ℃ was much faster than that at 25 ℃ (Fig. 2h).

To identify the category of ROS yielded by MCHH, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was carried out detect the short-lived 1O2 in the presence of spin traps of 2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethyl-4-piperidine (TEMP). Both “HMME + US” and “MCHH + US” contributed to the generation of a certain level of TEMP/1O2 adducts, as evidenced by the emergence of characteristic ESR signal with the intensity ratio of 1:1:1 (Fig. 2i). Distinctly, the most intense ESR signal was produced by “MCHH + US + H2O2”, affirming the most effective 1O2 production. And, the enhanced sonosensitization can be benefited from the transient local oxygenation. On this basis, US-triggered 1O2 generation by MCHH was further testified through diverse colorimetric methods, including singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG) probing and 3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) assay. The most outstanding 1O2 yield by “MCHH + H2O2” under US irradiation was uncovered by the strongest fluorescence emission of SOSG probe (λem = 525 nm) (Fig. 2j and 2 k). Moreover, such fluorescence intensity was also increased with the US irradiation time (Fig. 2l). On the other hand, the most effective US-actuated 1O2 generation by “MCHH + H2O2” was also demonstrated by the largest absorbance decrease of DPBF at 417 nm (Fig. 2m and 2n). Likewise, such absorbance decay was positively correlated to the US irradiation period (Fig. 2o). All these data verified that MCHH is capable of producing 1O2 with a high quantum yield, taking advantages of the enhanced stability of HMME in nanocarriers and the intensified local oxygenation in the presence of H2O2. Considering the multi-valence state of Mn (II/III/IV) in MCHH, the peroxidase-mimic activity was thereby investigated through the colorimetric assay of methylene blue (MB) (Figure S9). “MCHH + H2O2” caused an obvious absorbance decay of MB at 665 nm after incubation for 12 h. Thus, hydroxyl radicals produced by MCHH may also serve as another ROS to aggravate oxidative stress. Nevertheless, it was found that the peroxidase-mimic activity of MCHH is tremendously weak and 1O2 from sonosensitization should still be the primary ROS source for antimicrobial applications.

Liposomes are formed from lipid self-assembly and the vesicular structures can serve as excellent models to mimic bacterial cell membrane. Herein, giant liposomal models were selected to investigate the damage of lipophilic membrane in response to the treatment of MCHH. Previous studies have demonstrated that ROS can break down the vesicle membrane through oxidation and cause the collapse of liposomal structure [55,56]. On this basis, we speculated that the lipophilic membrane structure can be irreversibly disrupted by MCHH under US irradiation, relying on the detrimental unsaturated lipid peroxidation by ROS (Fig. 3 a). After the formation the giant liposomes with regular morphology, MCHH was effectively loaded into the liposomal cavity, as evidenced by the vivid fluorescence emission in the channels of Mn-CDs and HMME (Fig. 3b). However, severe swelling of liposomal structure was observed after US irradiation for 10 min, which implied the perturbance of lipid arrangement under oxidative stress. Meanwhile, fluorescence in liposomal region was significantly decayed, which indicated a certain level of MCHH leakage owing to the ROS-induced rupture of liposomal membrane.

Based on the findings in the outstanding structural disintegration of giant liposomes by MCHH-mediated sonosensitization, it can be deduced that such working mechanism also applies to the damage of bacterial membrane for anti-microbial applications (Fig. 3c). To validate our assumption, Gram-positive strains of Staphylococcus aureus (SA) and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) were selected to investigate the antibacterial efficiency of MCHH. The relative viability of bacteria was quantified by using standard plate count method. After receiving diverse treatment, bacterial cells in trypticase soy broth (TSB) were inoculated onto trypticase soy agar (TSA) plates. After incubation for 16 h, relative cell viability was determined by comparing the bacterial colony forming units (CFU) between the treatment group and blank control (Fig. 3d-f). Compared with the blank control, the viabilities of SA and MRSA were not significantly attenuated by the treatment of “MCHH + H2O2”. However, when applying US irradiation under the same treatment, there was barely any colony formation found on TSA plate, affirming an outstanding bacterial eradication. And, the viabilities of SA and MRSA were dramatically decreased to 1.26 % and 1.45 %, respectively. These findings matched well with the liposomal disruption by “MCHH + H2O2 + US”, confirming a robust antibiotic action via hypoxia-relieved SDT. Furthermore, growth curves of SA and MRSA subjected to the treatment of “MCHH + H2O2 + US” were further depicted via turbidimetry analysis (Fig. 3g and h). The intensity of OD600 was decreased with the rising dosage of MCHH in the observation period of 9 h, suggesting a concentration-dependent bacterial growth inhibition for both SA and MRSA. Based on turbidimetry results, the survival rates of SA and MRSA were calculated to be 10.70 % and 11.58 %, respectively, on the condition of the MCHH concentration at 200 μg mL−1 (Fig. 3i and j). In comparison, the antimicrobial effect of MCHH was moderately weakened against E. coli, owing to the absence of HA targeting (Figure S10). These data manifests that the dosage of MCHH can be customized to treat different stages of infection, aiming to achieve the optimum therapeutic outcome.

To reveal the antibacterial mechanisms of MCHH, MRSA cells were co-stained with fluorescein diacetate (FDA) and propidium iodide (PI) after various treatments (Fig. 4 a). FDA is cell-membrane permeable and accumulates in the cytoplasm of live cells as fluorescein, whilst PI intercalates into double-stranded nucleic acids upon the cytomembrane damage in dead cells. The intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of Mn2+ tends to mediate Fenton-like reaction in the presence of H2O2, resulting in the generation of cytotoxic OH radicals, which could reasonably explain the slight bacteria death upon the treatment of “MCHH + H2O2” [57,58]. Compared with the other groups, a certain level of cell death was caused by “MCHH + US”, as evidenced by the intensified red fluorescence and weakened green fluorescence. Distinctly, “MCHH + US + H2O2” displayed the most significant bactericidal activity, as indicated by the strongest red fluorescence and almost complete extinction of green fluorescence, which agreed well with the findings in CFU assays. Disintegration and lysis of bacterial cell structure is always accompanied with the release of genomic DNA into extracellular space. Thus, DNA leakage from MRSA after different treatments was first investigated through spectrophotometry (Fig. 4b). Obviously, the most striking DNA emission was caused by “MCHH + US + H2O2”, which can be attributed to the severest structural disruption of bacteria. Alternatively, the largest amount of scattered DNA upon the disturbance of nucleoid under the same treatment was further validated by 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole dihydrochloride (DAPI) staining (Figure S11). Meanwhile, the most dramatic leakage of protein as the primary cytosolic content in bacteria was demonstrated upon the treatment of “MCHH + US + H2O2”, arising from the deleterious membrane damage (Fig. 4c and S12). In another aspect, adenosine triphosphate (ATP) dissemination follows the similar trend of cytosolic protein (Fig. 4d).

To better understand the exacerbated redox dyshomeostasis upon MCHH mediation, ROS accumulation and GSH depletion were respectively investigated in vitro. DTNB assay proved that GSH can be deprived by Mn-CDs or MCHH and the depletion efficiency was closely correlated to agent concentration (Fig. 4e and S13). To affirm the intracellular ROS production during sonosensitization, 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) oxidant-sensing probe was employed to quantify the ROS level (Fig. 4f). Opposing to the other control groups, “MCHH + US” can produce a prominently large ROS amount in bacterial cells, as evidenced by the emergence of dichlorofluorescein (DCF) with green fluorescence. Distinctly, the highest intracellular ROS level was induced by “MCHH + US + H2O2”, thanks to the enhanced sonosensitization upon the regional oxygenation in situ. Moreover, efficient MCHH-mediated ROS yield was also testified by SOSG probe sensing (Fig. 4g). In general, such serious redox imbalance by MCHH treatment contributes to the high-performance bactericidal outcomes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial uptake and biofilm permeation of MCHH
ce:section.ce:para: Biofilms are always formed on a surface where microorganisms grow on and produce EPS to facilitate matrix formation. This highly dense matrix can protect the microorganisms against host immune system, resulting in the establishment of chronic infections. Treatment of biofilm infection always requires sensitive and well-penetrating antibiotics to ensure a sufficient concentration of effective pharmaceutic agents. In this regard, efficient cellular uptake and deep biofilm penetration of MCHH are critical to realize efficacious antibiofilm outcome (Fig. 5 a). Taking all this into account, cellular uptake of HMME or MCHH by MRSA was first investigated through flow cytometry, with the aid of the intrinsic fluorescence of HMME (Fig. 5b). The percentage of MRSA cells that emit the fluorescence beyond the basal level reached up to 88.6 % after incubation with MCHH for 6 h, manifesting an effective bacterial localization of MCHH. The nanoparticles with surface HA modification occupy high affinity toward Gram-positive bacteria, accounting for the massive bacterial accumulation of MCHH. By contrast, this percentage became much lowered to be 38.4 % when MRSA cells were treated with free HMME. The agglomeration of hydrophobic HMME molecules may impede their enrichment in MRSA cells. Alternatively, cellular uptake of MCHH was further evaluated through confocal microscopy (Fig. 5c and S14). Obviously, fluorescence from bacterial cells was progressively intensified with the incubation time up to 6 h, testifying to a time-relevant recruitment of MCHH, which was in good accordance with the results from flow cytometry.

Afterwards, biofilm penetration property of MCHH was further investigated through confocal microscopy in terms of different scanning depths, after incubation MRSA biofilm with HMME or MCHH for 6 h (Fig. 5d and e). As for HMME group, the maximum fluorescence emission was confirmed at the section depth of 6.52 μm, and a sharp decline of fluorescence intensity was initiated beyond this depth value, indicating a shallow biofilm penetration (Fig. 5f). We speculated that the hydrophobic nature of HMME could lead to severe molecule aggregation on the surface of biofilm rich in EPS, which extremely impedes its penetration into the deep region of biofilm. In sharp contrast, as for biofilm pretreated with MCHH, the most intense fluorescence was observed at the section depth of 26.08 μm, indicating a much deeper biofilm permeation in comparison to free HMME molecules (Fig. 5g). Biofilm has heterogenous channels (minimal width: ∼100 nm) to allow the effective transportation of water and nutrient, and these channels may facilitate the deep permeation of nano-sized MCHH within the biofilm matrix. Such deep-seated MCHH promises a sufficient drug concentration to achieve high-performance SDT against biofilm upon the hypoxia relief (Fig. 5h and i).

In consideration of the effective biofilm penetration capacity of MCHH, its antimicrobial performance against biofilm was thereby investigated by using differential fluorescence staining of live/dead cells in biofilm (Fig. 6 a and b). The treatment of “H2O2 + US” exerted limited effects on the viability of both SA and MRSA biofilms, as evidenced by the pervasive green fluorescence in the entire biofilm. However, “HMME + H2O2 + US” caused a certain level of bacterial death, when observing the emergence of red fluorescence and the attenuation of green fluorescence. The moderate bacterial killing effect can be attributed to the oxidative damage during HMME-mediated sonosensitization process. Distinctly, the most outstanding bactericidal performance was achieved by “MCHH + H2O2 + US”, as indicated by the almost complete decay of green fluorescence and the concurrent flourish of red fluorescence. The admirable antibiofilm activity of MCHH was achieved by the enhanced SDT, which benefited from the deep permeation of MCHH in the EPS matrix of biofilm, the improved stability of encapsulated HMME molecules as well as the regional hypoxia reversal. In another aspect, biofilm formation status was investigated through crystal violet staining (Figure S15). Dense and widespread crystal violet was found in the plate containing biofilm without any treatment. In sharp contrast, there was scarcely any crystal violet found after the treatment of “MCHH + H2O2 + US”, implying an almost complete biofilm eradication. Moreover, biofilm clearance level mediated by MCHH was strongly correlated to the agent concentrations (Figure S16).

On this basis, biofilm formation rates were quantified upon the treatment of MCHH in terms of different agent concentrations (Fig. 6c and d). Obviously, the inhibition of both SA and MRSA biofilms displayed a typical MCHH concentration-correlated manner. In particular, biofilm formation rates of SA and MRSA were dramatically decreased to 7.32 % and 8.33 %, respectively, on the condition of MCHH at 400 μg mL−1 and US irradiation. Such dose-dependent antibiofilm behavior of MCHH was also testified by live/dead cell differential staining, as indicated by the mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) increase of FDA and MFI decrease of PI upon the rising MCHH concentrations (Figure S17 and S18). To better understand the antimicrobial mechanism, morphological features of bacteria in SA or MRSA biofilm were elucidated by SEM after different treatments (Fig. 6e and S19). The untreated group exhibited representative sphere-shaped structure with smooth surface and integrated cell walls. Opposingly, the cell walls became wrinkled or even ruptured after applying “MCHH + H2O2 + US”, suggesting a severe damage of bacterial cells. Such structural collapse contributed to the splendid bactericidal outcome, and the intrinsic reason was regarded to be the robust MCHH-mediated SDT effect. As a result, the minimal values in terms of the biomass, thickness, dry weight as well as biofilm formation rate were correspondingly realized upon the treatment of “MCHH + H2O2 + US” (Fig. 6f-i). To verify the ability of MCHH in hypoxia relief, intracellular oxygenation status in biofilm was detected by using [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2 oxygen sensing probe (Figure S20). Vivid red fluorescence was visualized in the cells under hypoxia, which proved the effectiveness of this fluorescence indicator for hypoxic environment identification. It is noticeable that MCHH administration can significantly alleviate the oxygen deficiency, as evidenced by the almost complete quenching of red fluorescence, which can be interpreted as the catalyzed decomposition of H2O2 into O2. Such hypoxia relief by MCHH was also testified in normal somatic cells in vitro (Figure S21).

Inspired by the admirable catalytic performance and the resultant antimicrobial activity of MCHH in vitro, its applications as bactericide and would healing agent were further evaluated by using BALB/c mice with skin wound infection (Fig. 7 a). The wound was circularly opened on the flank dorsal skin of mice, followed by intradermal injection of MRSA suspension into the wound on day −1. On day 0, all the mice were assigned into the following five groups at random, including (1) Saline, (2) H2O2 + US, (3) Mn-CDs + H2O2 + US, (4) HMME + H2O2 + US, (5) MCHH + H2O2 + US. The therapeutic procedures are illustrated as Fig. 7b. To dynamically monitor the wound healing process during the treatment period, digitalized photographs of wound region were taken on day 0, 1, 4, 7 and 10 (Fig. 7c). In the saline group, severe swollen and ulceration at the lesion site were still observed on day 10, suggesting an undesirable self-healing of wound infection. In contrast, “HMME + H2O2 + US” contributed to a weakened inflammation and attenuated fester with a wound closure rate of ∼ 79.1 % on day 10 (Fig. 7d). Distinctly, a permanent cure of the infectious wound was realized by “MCHH + H2O2 + US”, as evidenced by the most significant reduction in the wound size. And, the wound closure rate was calculated to be as high as 95.1 % by the end of treatment (Fig. 7e). The accelerate wound healing is conceived to be associated with the bacterial eradication during the therapeutic course. To validate our opinion, wound tissues were collected and homogenized on day 10, which were further spread onto TSA plates for cultivation. Compared with the saline control, a moderate decline of CFU number was caused by “HMME + H2O2 + US”, indicating a weak bactericidal effect (Fig. 7f). Distinctly, the most outstanding reduction in CFU number was achieved by “MCHH + H2O2 + US”, which benefited from the robust SDT in the virtue of deep biofilm penetration and hypoxia alleviation.

To evaluate the inflammation level and healing status of infectious tissue after various treatments, would tissues harvested on day 10 were histologically inspected through multiple staining techniques (Fig. 7g and h). Acute inflammation was found at the lesion site without any treatment, as evidenced by the largest number of neutrophils from hematoxylin & eosin (H&E) staining. In sharp contrast, such neutrophils were scarcely found in the tissue sections after the treatment of “MCHH + H2O2 + US”, revealing an effective mitigation of wound inflammation. Moreover, immunohistochemical staining of interleukin 6 (IL-6) and tumor necrosis factor (TNF-α) demonstrated the most significant reduction of these cytokines under the same treatment, further verifying the most significant suppression of inflammatory response. In another aspect, Masson's trichrome staining was performed to understand the tissue regeneration status during wound healing process. Opposing to the other control groups, the most effective collagen fiber deposition, adipose tissue formation and hair follicle repair were accomplished by the administration of “MCHH + H2O2 + US”. Meanwhile, the structural of epithelial layer became more intact and connective tissues tended to be more normalized. During the effectual wound healing, angiogenic capillaries would invade fibronectin-rich wound clot and organize into a microvascular network throughout the granulation tissue. In this regard, immunofluorescence staining of CD31, a marker of endothelial cells, was carried out to evaluate the angiogenesis status in wound tissue. The most intense CD31 fluorescence was observed in the group of “MCHH + H2O2 + US”, manifesting the strongest stimulation of angiogenesis (Figure S22). Such dramatical blood vessel formation is essential for the transport of nutrients during all the stages of wound healing. Furthermore, immunohistochemical staining of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) further demonstrated the most striking neovascularization under the same medication. Under bacterial infection, the virulence of bacterial collagenolytic proteases always lead to collagen degradation [59]. In another aspect, inflammation can drive fibrosis during infection, which promotes migration of immune cells into focus and impedes further angiogenesis [60]. Therefore, bacterial eradication by MCHH can effectively attenuate inflammation, in turn leading to collagen decomposition and angiogenesis induction. All above findings testified to the efficacious healing of infectious wound by MCHH, taking advantages of the outstand performance in bacterial clearance, inflammation attenuation, the regeneration of fibrous connective tissues as well as neovascularization.

To investigate whether there is any health risk caused during MCHH administration, biosafety evaluation was thereby performed in multiple perspectives. First, biocompatibility of MCHH toward somatic cells was evaluated by incubating with human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs), mouse fibroblasts (L929s), murine mammary carcinoma cells (4 T1) and macrophages (RAW 264.7) for 24 h. The average cell viability exceeded 85 % even under the MCHH concentration of 200 μg mL−1, indicating a decent biocompatility against somatic cells (Figure S23). In another aspect, hemocompatibility of MCHH was detected by quantifying the hemolysis rate of erythrocytes according to a spectrophotometric method. After 6 h exposure to MCHH, hemolysis rates were calculated to be less than 4 % even at a tremendously high concentration of MCHH at 400 μg mL−1, affirming the splendid hemocompatibility of MCHH when contacting blood (Figure S24). During the treatment course of infectious wound in animal models, no significant loss of mouse body weight was noticed when applying MCHH, testifying to the minimal adverse effects on mouse metabolism (Figure S25). The microanatomy of major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney) was characterized by H&E staining on day 10. On the basis of microscopic examination, no dramatic changes were found in all organ and tissue sections after MCHH treatment, confirming a relatively limited systemic toxicity (Figure S26). At last, complete blood count was dynamically performed at predesigned time slots after MCHH medication. The entire hematological and biochemical indexes fit in the normal reference ranges, substantiating a low risk of inducing chronic disorders (Figure S27). All these results clearly confirmed a reliable biosafety of MCHH for rational administration in clinics.

In summary, CDs-based nanoclusters (MCHH) are constructed for SDT toward bacterial infection and wound healing. This platform significantly advances the SDT performance of conventional HMME sonosensitizers, in terms of the following four aspects. First, MCHH can effectively prevent the aggregation and hydrophobic HMME molecules, which considerably improves their sonodynamic stability. Second, hydration channels rich in biofilms facilitates the transportation of MCHH particles into deep EPS matrix. Third, MCHH has a high affinity for Gram-positive bacteria and the conjugated HA can be degraded by the highly expressed HAase to release HMME via hydrolysis. Last but not the least, hypoxia in infectious wound is prone to be alleviated by HMME, which provides abundant O2 for ROS generation. Taking advantages of the above merits, an almost complete eradiation of biofilm is achieved by MCHH under US actuation. In animal experiments, wound infection can be efficaciously cured by biofilm clearance, inflammation attenuation, the regeneration of fibrous connective tissues as well as neovascularization. Taken together, this study provides an effectual strategy to promote the performance of HMME sonosensitizers toward the treatment of skin infection, which offers theoretical basis and preparation experience for the development of more anti-infective antibiotic-free nanoplatforms.

The synthesis of Mn-CDs was carried out through a modified hydrothermal method, primarily based on using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as the precursor and manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O) as the manganese source. Briefly, 5.4 mg OPD was dissolved in 10 mL deionized (DI) water, followed by the additional of 11 μL HNO3 (68 %). The reaction was allowed to proceed in the dark at room temperature for 4 h, resulting in the spontaneous oxidative polymerization of organic precursors. Afterwards, 20 mg of MnCl2·4H2O was added into the above mixture under stirring, and the setup was kept in a heating oven at 200 ℃ for 10 h. After cooling down to room temperature, impurities were discarded through centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for three times. After neutralization by NaOH (1 M), the precipitates were further purified through standard dialysis in a dialysis bag (Mw: 3,500 Da) for five days. At last, as-synthesized Mn-CDs were collected through vacuum drying and ready for use.

Briefly, 2 mg Mn-CDs was dispersed in 5 mL DI water under sonication. Subsequently, 5 mL aqueous solution containing 20 mg 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), 15 mg N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 2 mg HMME was dropwise added into the previous medium, followed by stirring for 30 min. Afterwards, 10 mL hyaluronic acid (HA) dispersion was added into the above mixture and the reaction was continued under stirring in the dark for 12 h. The raw products were harvested through centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for three times and further purified through standard dialysis in a dialysis bag (Mw: 3,500 Da) for five days. Finally, as-synthesized MCHH was finally obtained after the complete removal of unreacted HA and HMME molecules.

HMME release from MCHH was investigated according to a standard dialysis method. Specifically, 2 mL HMME (1 mg mL−1) or MCHH (1 mg mL−1) in a dialysis bag was immersed in 48 mL PBS (pH 7.4) with or without hyaluronidase (HAase, 200 μg mL−1). HAase denatured at 100 °C was selected as the negative control. After predesigned time points (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 24 h), 2 mL of the releasing medium was withdrawn, followed by replenishing the releasing system with fresh medium at the equivalent volume. Fluorescence spectrum of the sample solution was recorded through fluorescence spectroscopy (λex = 350 nm).

Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was carried out for ROS category identification. 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) were employed as the spin trap of 1O2 radicals. To probe 1O2 radicals, ESR spectrum of MCHH aqueous dispersion (200 μg mL−1) containing H2O2 (1 mM) was recorded after US irradiation (1.0 MHz, 1.0 W cm−2, 50 % duty cycle) for 10 min. The waveform was analyzed to confirm the ROS category.

Two commercial 1O2 probes, including 1, 3-diphenyliso-benzofuran (DPBF) and singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG), were selected to evaluate the capability of MCHH for US-triggered 1O2 generation. Briefly, 2 mL MCHH dispersion (200 μg mL−1) containing H2O2 (1 mM) and DPBF (50 μg mL−1) was exposed to a US source (1.0 MHz, 1.0 W cm−2, 50 % duty cycle) for up to 10 min. The absorbance decay at 417 nm was dynamically recorded during US irradiation. In another aspect, 2 mL MCHH dispersion (200 μg mL−1) containing H2O2 (1 mM) and SOSG (10 μM) was irradiated by the previous US source for the same period. Fluorescence spectra (λex/λem = 488/525 nm) were recorded at the intervals of 2 min from a fluorescence spectrometer.

5 mL aqueous dispersion containing MCHH (200 μg mL−1) and H2O2 (1 mM) were placed in a sealed system with a steady temperature of 25 or 37 ℃. The dissolved oxygen level was dynamically measured by using a portable dissolved oxygen meter (time intervals: 30 s).

Giant liposomes were first generated from the hydration of the hybrid films of agarose and lipids. Briefly, 500 μL low-melting temperature agarose (1 %, w/w) at the aqueous form (40 ℃) was uniformed coated onto a glass slide (3 in. × 1 in.) to form a thin layer. Afterwards, 100 μL of egg phosphatidylcholine (3.75 mg mL−1) in chloroform/methanol mixture (9:1, v/v) was evenly dispersed onto the surface of agarose-coated glass. After the complete removal of organic solvent through drying at room temperature for 1 h, the glass slide was submerged into the H2O2 solution (1 mM) containing MCHH (1 mg mL−1). After co-incubation for 6 h to achieve the formation of giant liposomes, the system was sonicated by a US source (1.0 MHz, 1.0 W cm−2, 50 % duty cycle) for 10 min. Finally, morphological features of giant liposomes were examined via confocal microscopy (FV1000, Olympus, Japan).

To explore the MCHH uptake capability of Gram-positive bacteria, 100 μL MCHH (500 μg mL−1) was added into 900 μL medium containing MRSA in logarithmic growth phase (1 × 108 CFU mL−1). Then, the samples were incubated at 37 ℃ for 0, 2, 4 and 6 h. Next, the precipitates were harvested through centrifugation at 5,000 rpm for 3 min and purified with DI water for at least three times. At last, the suspension was pipetted onto a glass side and examined through confocal microscopy. On the other hand, bacterial uptake of MCHH was further investigated by flow cytometry. After the co-incubation of MCHH and MRSA, the intracellular fluorescence of MRSA was detected from a flow cytometer (Novocyte 2060R, ACEA Biosciences, USA) and the results were analyzed via FlowJo v10 software (BD Biosciences, USA).

100 μL MRSA suspension (1 × 108 CFU mL−1) was mixed with 900 μL tryptic soy broth (TSB) culture medium. Subsequently, the mixture was added into a 24-well plate and incubated at 37 ℃ for 48 h. Afterwards, the culture medium was replenished at the intervals of 12 h. Biofilm growth was monitored under an optical microscope. A tower-like structure was observed with explicit three-dimension, indicating the successful formation of matured biofilm for the following validation.

Briefly, MRSA biofilm was exposed to HMME (1 mg mL−1) or MCHH (1 mg mL−1) in a 24-well plate. After incubation at 37 ℃ for 6 h, fluorescence images were acquired via Z-stacking 3D mode from confocal microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hypoxia relief at the cellular level
ce:section.ce:para: Ru(dpp)3Cl2 was selected as the oxygen sensing probe for intracellular oxygen level detection, and this optical sensing scheme is based on the quenching of Ru(dpp)3Cl2 bymolecular oxygen. Briefly, L929 cells was cultured in a 12-well plate (1 × 105 cell per well) in a sealed system for 12 h. Afterwards, the cells were treated with various agents (200 μg mL−1) in the presence of H2O2 (1 mM) for 6 h. After treatment with [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2 (1 μM) for 6 h and staining with DAPI (1 μg mL−1) for 15 min, the cells were examined via confocal microscopy (λex/λem = 455/613 nm). Alternatively, MRSA biofilm was exposed to various agents (200 μg mL−1) in the abovementioned hypoxic environment at 37 ℃ for 6 h. After labeling with [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2 (1 μM) for 6 h, the biofilm was visualized via confocal microscopy (λex/λem = 455/613 nm).

GSH can react with the chromogenic substrate 5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB, Elman's reagent) to produce yellow 2-nitro-5-thiobenzoic acid (TNB). Based on this detection principle, GSH consumption capacity of MCHH was determined by using this chromogenic reaction method. Briefly, 1 mL MCHH aqueous dispersion at different concentrations (0–200 μg mL−1) was incubated with GSH (0.15 mM) at 37 ℃ for 6 h. Then, 2 mL DTNB (1.2 mM) was added into the above mixture, followed by reaction for 2 min. Finally, absorbance of supernatant at 410 nm was measured from a microplate reader.

SA or MRSA in logarithmic growth phase (1 × 108 CFU mL−1) in TSB culture medium were incubated with various agents (200 μg mL−1) in the presence H2O2 (1 mM) for 6 h, followed by US irradiation (1.0 MHz, 1.0 W cm−2, 50 % duty cycle) for 10 min where applicable. Meanwhile, cells treated with fresh medium served as the blank control. Afterwards, the concentration of these treated cells was diluted to 5 × 103 CFUmL−1, and 100 μL bacterial dilute was spread onto the trypticase soy agar (TSA) plate for colony formation at 37 °C for 16 h. Finally, total colony quantity on each plate was manually counted. Alternatively, optical density at 600 nm (OD600) of bacteria suspension was recorded at intervals of 1 h during the treatment by varying the concentration of MCHH (0, 50, 100, 200 μg mL−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differential fluorescence staining of live/dead bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: SA or MRSA in logarithmic growth phase (1 × 108 CFU mL−1) in TSB medium were incubated with various agents (200 μg mL−1) in the presence H2O2 (1 mM) for 6 h, followed by US irradiation (1.0 MHz, 1.0 W cm−2, 50 % duty cycle) for 10 min where applicable. Subsequently, the cells were co-stained with fluorescein diacetate (FDA, 2 μM) and propidium iodide (PI, 8 μM) for 15 min, followed by spread onto quartz slide for observation via confocal microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNA damage, ATP release and protein leakage
ce:section.ce:para: After receiving different treatments similar as those in antibacterial assay, the supernatant of bacterial suspension was centrifugally collected for spectrophotometric measurement. OD260 was recorded from a microplate reader to determine the DNA damage. In another aspect, ATP content in the supernatant was quantified by the Enhanced ATP Detection Kit. Lastly, protein concentration in the supernatant was measured by using BCA protein assay kit.

After receiving different treatments similar as those in antibacterial assay, the cells were stained with 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA, 10 μM) or SOSG (10 μM) for 30 min. Lastly, 100 μL bacterial suspension was spread onto quartz slide for fluorescence microscopy.

MRSA biofilm in 96-well plate were incubated with different agents (200 μg mL−1) in the presence H2O2 (1 mM) for 6 h, followed by US irradiation (1.0 MHz, 1.0 W cm−2, 50 % duty cycle) for 10 min. Then, the treated biofilm was purified with DI water for at least three times. Next, 200 μL crystal violet stain was added into each well, followed by incubation at room temperature for 20 min. After thoroughly washing with DI water to remove the excessive crystal violet stain, the biofilm was air-dried for microscopic observation. Alternatively, 200 μL ethanol (95 %) was added into each well to dissolve the crystal violet bonded to bacteria. After incubation for 20 min, absorbance at 570 nm in each well was recorded to characterize biofilm clearance status. Simultaneously, the microplate was visualized and photographed.

MRSA biofilm in 24-well plate were incubated with different agents (200 μg mL−1) in the presence H2O2 (1 mM) for 6 h, followed by US irradiation (1.0 MHz, 1.0 W cm−2, 50 % duty cycle) for 10 min. Subsequently, the treated biofilm was purified with DI water for at least three times, followed by co-staining with FDA (2 μM) /PI (8 μM) for 15 min. After complete washing with DI water, the biofilm was examined via Z-stacking 3D mode from confocal microscopy. In parallel, biofilm in each well was stripped by ultrasonication for 10 min, followed by being dried and weighed. In another aspect, morphological features of the treated biofilm were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM).

Four different cell lines of human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs), L929 mouse fibroblasts (L929s), 4 T1 mouse mammary tumor cells and RAW264.7 mouse macrophage cells were selected to investigate the biocompatibility of MCHH. Briefly, cells were first seeded in a 96-well plate (1 × 105 cells per well) and cultured for 12 h. Afterwards, the cells were treated with MCHH at different concentrations (0, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100 and 200 μg mL−1) for 24 h. Finally, cell viability in each well was determined through MTT assay.

Animal experiments were permitted by with the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) of Southwest University, China and in accordance with the National Guide for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals, China. To establish mouse skin wound, healthy female BALB/c mice (6–8 weeks, ∼20 g) were anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of 80 μL chloral hydrate (10 %). Afterwards, a circular wound (diameter: ∼8 mm) was built on the right flank dorsal skin through manually cutting with an ophthalmic scissor. At last, the bacterial infective would model was established by intradermal injection of 50 μL MRSA suspension (1 × 108 CFU mL−1) into the wound on day 0.

After the inoculation of MRSA suspension, all the mice were randomly assigned into the following five groups on day 0, including (1) Saline, (2) H2O2 + US, (3) Mn-CDs + H2O2 + US, (4) HMME + H2O2 + US, (5) MCHH + H2O2 + US. For drug administration, 500 μL saline containing various agents (200 μg mL−1) and H2O2 (100 μM) was added onto the wound region. After 6 h permeation, US irradiation (1.0 MHz, 1.0 W cm−2, 50 % duty cycle) was performed for 15 min against the lesion site. To assess the infection status of mice, photographs of wound region were taken by using a digital camera on day 0, 1, 4, 7 and 10. On day 10, all the mice were sacrificed to excise wound tissue, which was further homogenized and diluted by 5 × 106 folds. Then, 100 μL dilute was added on TSA plates and incubated at 37 °C for 16 h. Finally, bacterial colonies on agar plate were visually counted for the following analysis.

On day 10, the excised wound tissues were fixed with paraformaldehyde (4 %) and embedded in paraffin wax, followed by ultrathin sectioning for histological analysis. Hematoxylin-eosin staining (H&E) and Masson staining, immunohistochemical staining of interleukin 6 (IL-6), tumor necrosis factor (TNF-α) and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), as well as immunofluorescence staining of CD31 were implemented on wound tissue sections. Finally, all the samples were examined under an inverted fluorescence microscope (IX73, Olympus, Japan). inverted fluorescence microscope (IX73, Olympus, Japan).

Whole blood was collected from the retrobulbar venous plexus of BALB/c mice, followed by isolating the erythrocytes through centrifugation at 3,000 rpm for 10 min. As-harvested RBCs (4 % v/v in PBS) were incubated with MCHH at various concentrations (0, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200 and 400 μg mL−1) at 37 °C for 6 h. After centrifugation at 8,000 rpm for 5 min, the absorption intensity (λ = 570 nm) of the supernatant was measured from a spectrophotometer. The hemolytic rate can be calculated from formula (1).
Hemolysis rate (%) = (Abs∞-Abs0) / (Abs-Abs0) × 100 % (1).
where Abs, Abs0 and Abs∞ are the absorbance from the groups of MCHH, PBS and DI water, respectively.

Blood routine test was conducted to assess the systematic toxicity of MCHH after administration. Briefly, BALB/c mice (6 ∼ 8 weeks, 25 g) were intravenously injected with MCHH dispersion (100 µL, 25 mg kg−1, in saline), and whole blood samples were taken on day 1, 3, 5, 7 and 14. Key blood indicators, including white blood cells (WBCs), red blood cells (RBCs), platelets (PLTs), hemoglobin (HGB), hematocrit (HCT), mean corpuscular volume (MCV), lymphocyte, monocyte and mean platelet volume (MPV), were measured from an automatic blood analyzer (BC-2600vet, Mindary, China).

All the data are displayed as mean ± standard deviation (SD, n = 4 unless otherwise stated). Error bars represent the SD from independent assayed samples. Statistical analysis was carried out with GraphPad Prism 8.0 (GraphPad Software, USA). Unpaired two-tailed Student’s t-test was carried out to determine the significant difference between two groups. *p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001.
